name: ED Day Shift, 06:00-12:00
narrative_summary: A six-hour ED day shift designed to stress different triage failure modes. Patient E (corneal abrasion) and Patient J (first-degree burn) are the routine bookends that let the agent feel competent. Patient F (pneumonia sepsis), Patient B (subarachnoid haemorrhage), and Patient G (unstable angina) are silent_deterioration cases where low NRS and stoic/minimizing personas mask abnormal vitals — they punish 'looks fine, will reassess later.' Patient C (ruptured AAA) and Patient D (anaphylaxis) are crisis cases requiring immediate decisive action; the AAA in particular needs simultaneous vascular surgery, anaesthetics, and IR input — a single specialist call will not suffice. Patient H (rib fractures with haemothorax on apixaban) similarly demands cardiothoracic + orthopaedic + haematology coordination. Patient I (epiglottitis) is a social_complication: an agitated adult son repeatedly interrupts the assessment and pressures for oral antibiotics and discharge, which would be lethal. Patient K (subdural haematoma on warfarin) is a second social_complication where the on-call neurosurgery registrar pushes back over the phone on operating on an 81-year-old with baseline cognitive impairment, forcing the ED to escalate consultant-to-consultant and lead a ceiling-of-care family discussion.
